added.name: Emotional Arc and Sequencing
added.description: Information must follow a specific order: acknowledge the situation or effort first (one sentence max), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters, then one concrete next step. Violating this sequence feels wrong even if all the pieces are present. This isn't just about completeness — it's about the reader feeling oriented before they're asked to act.

added.name: Clear, Single-Owner Action Items
added.description: When action is needed, name one owner with a deadline. Group asks like 'everyone please review' are non-actions. At the same time, don't over-specify — action items shouldn't assume org structure details the email has no business containing. The ask should be clear without being presumptuous about how the recipient's team works.

removed.name: Purposeful Openings
removed.description: Openings should name the situation immediately without filler — no 'announcing that an announcement is coming.' The opening should add framing the subject line doesn't already carry, not restate it. Before delivering hard news, one orienting sentence gives the reader a beat of footing so the message feels like a person talking, not a subject line repeated.

reworded.name: Reader-Centered Justification
reworded.old: When explaining a decision or making an ask, frame it from where the audience is sitting — including what they've already built, committed to, or been waiting on. Name the specific costs or dependencies they carry before making the case. The 'why' should land from their perspective, not from the sender's. Ordering matters: impact first, rationale second.
reworded.new: When explaining a decision or making an ask, frame the 'why' entirely from the reader's perspective — what they've committed to, planned around, or will now have to absorb. The sender's reasoning, standards, or values should not be the frame. If the email centers the sender's logic over the reader's situation, it reads as tone-deaf.

reworded.name: Operational Closings
reworded.old: The email should land on concrete next steps, deadlines, and accountability — not dissolve into gratitude or motivation. The reader should walk away knowing exactly what happens next. Once a concrete CTA is stated, the message ends — open-ended offers or soft follow-up invitations after the CTA dilute it.
reworded.new: The email ends on a concrete next step with a deadline and a clear owner. Once the CTA is stated, the email is over — no trailing sentiment, no 'thanks for your patience,' no open-ended offers to answer questions. The last thing the reader sees should be the action, not a feeling.

reworded.name: Direct, Human Tone
reworded.old: The message should read like a person talking, not a corporate memo. Avoid stock phrases and sentiment padding. Use plain, specific language and crisp comparative framing over hedged or abstract corporate vocabulary. The strongest signals of human tone are situation-specific word choices — language that could only have been written about this reader's situation, not recycled from any other email.
reworded.new: The message should read like a person talking, not a corporate memo. Avoid stock phrases, jargon, and sentiment padding. Use plain, specific language. The strongest signal of human tone is language that could only have been written about this reader's situation — not recycled from any other email.

reworded.name: Lean Information Density
reworded.old: Timelines and action items should read as a sequence of checkpoints — dates and milestones only. Strip role descriptions, process explanations, and details the audience already knows. Anything that doesn't sharpen clarity is noise that dilutes the signal.
reworded.new: Strip anything that doesn't sharpen clarity. Timelines list dates and outcomes, not process explanations. Don't re-explain things the audience already knows. Short paragraphs. Every sentence earns its place.

dimensions_changed.name: Reader-Centered Justification
removed_dims: Acknowledgment of impact comes before the justification, not after

dimensions_changed.name: Operational Closings
added_dims: Does not append open-ended offers ('happy to answer questions,' 'feel free to reach out') after a concrete CTA
Closing line is action-oriented and forward-looking — not backward-looking gratitude or encouragement
removed_dims: Escalation path or point of contact is stated explicitly, not left implied
Key milestones or deadlines are compressed into a tight closing sequence the reader retains
Does not append open-ended offers ('happy to answer questions,' 'feel free to reach out') after a concrete CTA — the action item is the last thing the reader sees

dimensions_changed.name: Direct, Human Tone
added_dims: Free of corporate jargon — no 'synergy,' 'leverage,' 'circle back,' 'alignment,' or similar filler
removed_dims: Key trade-offs or decisions are framed in clean, comparative terms rather than hedged explanations

dimensions_changed.name: Lean Information Density
added_dims: Paragraphs are short — no walls of text, no multi-sentence blocks that could be split or cut
removed_dims: Ownership is consolidated to a single person or channel rather than scattered across roles

reasoning: I made three main changes: First, I pulled the emotional arc and sequencing out into its own top-priority criterion because the order of information is the thing I care about most and it wasn't clearly captured before — it was scattered across other criteria. Second, I added a dedicated criterion for single-owner action items because the 'one clear owner' preference and the 'don't over-specify org structure' tension both matter and weren't represented at all. Third, I cleaned up the 'Operational Closings' criterion to be sharper about the forward-looking close requirement and dropped the 'closing_restates_sequence' dimension, which felt like over-engineering — I just want the email to end on the action.

reviewed_changes.original_text: Quick update — the feature you requested six months ago is finally ready and shipping next week. We know you've been waiting on this one, so we wanted to make sure you heard it from us first.
reviewed_changes.new_text: Six months is a long time to wait — the feature is finally ready.
reviewed_changes.reason: Emotional Arc and Sequencing (new criterion at priority #1, 'acknowledgment_first' dimension): The original blended acknowledgment and news into the same sentence and added a self-centered 'we wanted to make sure you heard it from us first.' The new opener isolates the acknowledgment in one clean sentence per the required sequence, and removes the sender-centered framing.

reviewed_changes.original_text: There's one thing you'll need to do on your end: your IT team will need to enable the feature once it's live. It won't activate automatically, so
reviewed_changes.new_text: It ships next week, but it won't activate automatically.
reviewed_changes.reason: Emotional Arc and Sequencing (new criterion, 'news_before_why' and 'why_before_next_step' dimensions): The news (ships next week) and the consequence (won't activate automatically) needed to be separated from the action item and placed before the CTA. Restructured so news lands first, consequence follows, then the ask.

reviewed_changes.original_text: looping them in before next week will keep you from losing time on launch day.
reviewed_changes.new_text: Your IT team will need to enable it on their end, and if that doesn't happen before launch, you'll lose time on day one.
reviewed_changes.reason: Direct, Human Tone (new 'no_jargon' dimension): 'Looping them in' is corporate jargon. Replaced with plain language. Also sharpens the consequence framing per Reader-Centered Justification (priority #2, 'names_audience_costs'): the burden to the reader is stated directly.

Clear, Single-Owner Action Items (new criterion at priority #4, 'single_owner' dimension): 'Your IT team' is a group ask that diffuses accountability. 'Your IT contact' points toward a single owner without over-specifying org structure.
reviewed_changes.user_feedback: I actually prefer 'IT team' here. 'IT contact' feels like I'm assuming they have a single point person, which might not be true — and it sounds weirdly specific in a way that could come off as presumptuous. 'IT team' is vague enough to let them figure out who owns it internally without me guessing at their org structure. The goal is one clear action, not one named person I don't know.

dimensions_changed.name: Direct, Human Tone
added_dims: No excessive exclamation marks — enthusiasm is conveyed through word choice, not punctuation

reasoning: I moved Operational Closings up to priority 3 — it's a dealbreaker for me when emails trail off with sentiment after the CTA, and the old priority 5 felt like it was being treated as a nice-to-have. I also swapped Clear Action Items down to 4 since it's important but downstream of getting the close right. The main addition was a dimension under Direct, Human Tone explicitly flagging excessive exclamation marks, which was missing entirely from the rubric even though it's a hard no for me.

dimensions_changed.name: Operational Closings
added_dims: No motivational platitudes closing the email — phrases like 'I know we can get through this together' or 'looking forward to great things ahead' are filler, not closes

reasoning: I made two targeted changes. First, I swapped the priority of 'Clear, Single-Owner Action Items' and 'Lean Information Density' — diffuse or unowned action items are a bigger failure mode for me than information bloat, so action item clarity should rank higher. Second, I added a dimension to 'Operational Closings' to explicitly call out motivational platitudes as a closing failure — the existing dimensions covered trailing sentiment and soft offers, but the platitude pattern ('I know we can get through this') is its own distinct problem that wasn't named.

reviewed_changes.original_text: Let's meet Thursday by noon to confirm the revised timeline and settle who owns alerts.
reviewed_changes.new_text: Confirm the revised timeline and which team owns alerts by Thursday at noon.
reviewed_changes.reason: Clear, Single-Owner Action Items (priority raised from #6 to #4, direct_verb_on_owner dimension): 'Let's meet' is a collective, soft framing that diffuses accountability. Replacing it with 'Confirm' lands the action verb directly on the recipients, making ownership unambiguous.
reviewed_changes.user_feedback: The new version is grammatically awkward — 'Confirm the revised timeline and which team owns alerts' reads like a fragment. I also want a single owner on this, not a group ask. Something like 'Can you confirm the revised timeline and alert ownership by Thursday noon?' with a named recipient works better. The original 'Let's meet' was soft, sure, but the fix went too far in the other direction and lost the human voice.
